Magic Compact enables lossless and aggressive context compression.Magic Compact enables aggressive, lossless context compression that dramatically reduces token costs and makes your coding plan go 2–3x further.

4 min read
Just now

--

Back in February, I was reverse engineering Claude Code’s source code from the minified JS bundles with OpenCode. Over a series of turns, we’d built up a map of the codebase: symbol mappings, the main conversation loop, reverse-engineered TUI modules, the permission system.
But whenever we’d dig deeper into some subsystem, the context window would fill up and force me to compact. And every time, OpenCode’s built-in compaction flattened everything we’d painstakingly mapped into a generic summary that captured maybe 10% of what mattered. Post-compaction, the agent quality dropped off a cliff. The assistant came out the other side starting from scratch, re-reading the same files, re-deriving the same conclusions.
After enough rounds of this, I built Magic Compact, a lossless context compression plugin for OpenCode. I’ve been using it daily for 4 months, and it’s made my coding plans last 2–3x longer and saved me thousands of dollars in token costs.
Today, I’m open sourcing it. Install now:
opencode plugin magic-compact --globalPress enter or click to view image in full size

Magic Compact preserves the conversation structure.Why Built-in Compaction Fails
OpenCode’s default compaction summarizes the entire conversation into one monolithic summary via a single prompt. But here’s the problem: A single summary cannot capture the working state of a long session; every tool call, every decision, every finding, every line of reasoning, every discovery, all get flattened together into a single Markdown summary.
So the model wakes up after compaction with no working memory of what it has done. It re-reads files, re-derives conclusions, redoing work. Worse, because each compaction degrades quality, you actively discouraged from compacting, so you’re stuck stretching the context window as far along as possible.
At large context sizes, each tool call burns through hundreds of thousands of stale and useless tokens, rapidly draining your usage limit, immediately killing your coding plan. When you’re working with 100k tokens in your context instead of 40k tokens, it’s only natural that you hit your usage limits in 2 hours instead of the usual 5.
The Idea Behind Magic Compact
Magic Compact takes a different approach: keep the conversation structure and quality intact and while compressing extremely aggressively.
User messages are preserved exactly. Old assistant turns get lean per-turn summaries that keep the thought process and decisions intact. Tool calls stay visible so the model knows what it did, while bulky tool I/O is omitted to a retrievable cache the agent can read back on demand.
Because the compression is lossless, the agent keeps its working memory. It knows what it explored, what it decided, what to do next. No re-thinking, no re-deriving, no wasted tokens.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Making Your Plan Last 3x Longer
Here’s where it matters. Because quality doesn’t degrade, you can run Magic Compact far more aggressively than built-in compaction to prune stale tool calls and thinking blocks. I run /magic-compact constantly: after finishing a feature, between phases of a plan, even mid-feature implementation.
Each compaction trims around 60–70% of the context window. Over a long session, that compounds, resulting in millions of cached reads saved over the implementation of your feature implementation. That translates to massively reduced plan usage.
I’ve probably saved hundreds of billions of cached read tokens over the last 4 months of daily use. That translates to thousands of dollars in token costs, and it makes the cheapest plans from Z.ai and OpenAI viable for serious, long-running work. My coding plans last 2–3x longer with aggressive compaction, letting me code longer than ever before on the same budget.
If you’ve ever hit your plan limit mid-session, try out Magic Compact.
Getting Started
You can install Magic Compact and use it in OpenCode today. Run the following command in your terminal:
opencode plugin magic-compact --globalThen, run /magic-compact [N] to compact, where N is the number of recent assistant turns to preserve (default 0). Run /magic-stats to see your cumulative token savings in the current conversation in real time.
Magic Compact has been fully open sourced. Feedback and contributions are welcome:
